title: Cherry pie, double-crust
subtitle: Sour cherries between two layers of shortcrust
excerpt: An American double-crust cherry pie: sour cherries thickened with cornflour and sugar, sealed under a lattice or full pastry top, and baked until the filling bubbles through the vents. One 23 cm pie, serving eight.
sourceNotes: Fannie Farmer, The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896), Project Gutenberg: documents the canonical American double-crust fruit pie. Mary Lincoln, Mrs. Lincoln's Boston Cook Book (1884), Project Gutenberg: gives the cornflour-thickened sour-cherry filling. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: documents the British covered fruit-pie tradition that runs in parallel.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze assembled unbaked pie whole, wrapped twice; bake from frozen at 180°C fan, adding 15 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the pastry up to 2 days ahead.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf — drops to 180°C after 20 minutes

recipeTools.notes: Or push a pen-cap or pastry-bag tip through each cherry to pop the stone.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wet-filling pies need a route for the steam to escape during the bake; otherwise the filling boils up against the top crust and bursts through unpredictably. The two standard vent strategies are a lattice top (open weave, vents itself) and a full top crust with five short cuts from the centre, fanning outward like spokes of a wheel.

content.text: A proper cherry pie sits between two layers of shortcrust pastry: a base lining a deep pie dish, and a top either as a full sealed lid or woven into a lattice. The filling is sour cherries thickened with cornflour and sweetened with caster sugar; a splash of almond extract underlines the cherry flavour.

Fresh sour cherries (morello or amarena) make the best pie. Frozen sour cherries work too — bake from frozen, no need to defrost. Sweet eating cherries lack the acid to balance the sugar; use them only when no sour fruit is available, and reduce the sugar by 30 g.

Rub the {{unsalted-butter}} into the {{plain-flour}}, {{caster-sugar}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} until the mix looks like coarse breadcrumbs with pea-sized butter pieces visible. Add the {{water}} a tablespoon at a time, stirring with a knife, until the dough just comes together. Divide in two (one slightly larger for the base), wrap flat, and chill an hour.

Mix the pitted {{cherries}} with the second {{caster-sugar}}, {{cornflour}}, juice of the {{lemon}} and {{almond-extract}}. Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Roll the larger pastry disc to a 30 cm round and line the pie dish. Tip the filling in, doming slightly in the centre. Roll the second disc and lay over the top (or cut into strips and weave a lattice — see
). Seal the edges and crimp. If using a full top, cut five short slashes from the centre out as steam vents.

Brush the top with the beaten {{eggs}} and scatter with {{demerara-sugar}}. Set the pie on a tray to catch drips. Bake at 200°C fan for 20 minutes, then drop to 180°C fan for another 30–35 minutes; the pastry should be deep gold and the filling should bubble visibly through the vents. Cool 30 minutes before slicing; the filling sets as it cools.
